narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've built a solid foundation with your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which stands above the typical 3.21 average, and an LSAT score of 36, close to the usual 36.70 mark seen among applicants. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, in your earlier school years, you ranked in the 5th decile for Year 1 grades and 4th decile for Year 3, both below the averages of around 5.7 and 5.5. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 41.3% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass on their first try. Among the key factors driving this decision, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward bar passage because it's stronger than most. Your LSAT score came in second, also helping your case by signaling solid potential for success. What tipped the scale most directly against you was your Year 3 decile ranking, the third-biggest factor overall, as its lower-than-average position pulled the prediction noticeably toward failure risk. Compared to typical students who get approved, your academic deciles are weaker, which outweighed your edges in GPA and LSAT despite your full-time commitment and stable background. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic man from a supportive family—it's the model highlighting patterns where mid-tier high school performance correlates with bar struggles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average or strengthening recent academics through post-bac courses to lift those decile perceptions. You could also target schools with holistic reviews that weigh your unique story more heavily. Gathering letters emphasizing your resilience and community ties might help reframe your profile. With targeted steps, you're well-positioned to turn this around and pursue your legal ambitions.
